title: BIN Attack Camouflaged Through Retailer Account Creation Velocity
narrative: Fraudsters exploit legitimate e-commerce signup flows to test 40-80 card numbers within a 90-minute window, masquerading as new customer registrations. Each transaction appears as a low-value ($0.50-$2.99) account verification charge or digital wallet add, typical of retailer onboarding processes. All attempts share identical P_emaildomain (temp-mail.org or similar disposable services), same addr1 postal code, and sequential card1 values suggesting systematic BIN enumeration. Transactions cluster in off-peak hours (txn_hour 2-5 AM) when fraud monitoring is reduced. The fraudster rotates through 8-12 distinct R_emaildomain values to avoid single-recipient flags, but dist1 remains constant at 0-15km from a consistent IP geolocation. Successful validations are followed 2-6 hours later by higher-value transactions ($150-$400) at different merchants using the same card1 values.
indicators: 40-80 transactions with sequential card1 values within 90-minute window, same addr1 and P_emaildomain
TransactionAmt consistently $0.50-$2.99, all flagged as ProductCD 'W' (digital wallet adds)
P_emaildomain matches disposable service (temp-mail.org, guerrillamail.com), R_emaildomain rotates across 8-12 domains
All transactions during txn_hour 2-5 AM, is_night=1, from same dist1 radius <15km
card1_txn_count_1day spikes to 40+ for new cards with card1_amt_zscore <0.5 on initial attempts
Validated card1 values reappear 2-6 hours later with TransactionAmt $150-$400 at unrelated MCCs
AVS mismatch rate >70% across the cohort despite shared addr1 postal code
summary: 40-80 small charges ($0.50-$2.99) hit the same retailer within 90 minutes during early morning hours (2-5 AM), all using sequential card numbers, the same shipping address, and disposable email domains. The validated cards then appear 2-6 hours later making larger purchases ($150-$400) at different merchants.
reasoning: Criminals are systematically testing stolen or generated card numbers through a retailer's account creation flow to identify which cards are active and valid, then immediately using the working cards for higher-value fraud before the victims notice.
indicator_explanations: Sequential card numbers are being tested from the same address and email
All amounts are tiny wallet-add tests checking if cards are live
Fraudster uses throwaway sender email but rotates through many receiving domains
Attack runs overnight from a tight geographic area during low-monitoring hours
Fresh cards suddenly see 40+ transactions despite amounts barely changing from baseline
Cards that passed validation return hours later for big-ticket fraud purchases
Most addresses fail verification despite sharing the same postal code marker
